structured.summary: This paper presents a comprehensive empirical study on the use of pretrained language models (PLMs) in continual learning (CL) for NLP. It benchmarks five PLMs combined with various CL methods across multiple datasets and settings, and provides detailed layer-wise analyses to understand forgetting and robustness.

strengths.point: Comprehensive benchmarking of multiple PLMs and CL methods across diverse datasets and settings.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.1 and Table 1 describe experiments involving 5 PLMs, 6 CL schemes, 3 datasets, and two incremental learning settings.

strengths.point: Detailed layer-wise probing methodology to analyze representational forgetting within PLMs.
strengths.evidence: Section 4 introduces a prototype-based probing method to evaluate layer-wise representativeness and forgetting.

strengths.point: Insightful findings on the relative robustness of PLMs and effectiveness of experience replay methods.
strengths.evidence: Sections 3.2 and 5 discuss that BERT is the most robust PLM and that rehearsal-based methods outperform regularization-based methods.

strengths.point: Identification of new research questions and directions based on empirical observations.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.3 and Section 6 outline open questions and future research inspired by the study's findings.

weaknesses.point: Lack of theoretical analysis or explanation for why certain PLMs or CL methods perform better.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper primarily focuses on empirical results and probing without providing theoretical insights or formal justification.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of domain-incremental or task-agnostic continual learning settings.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.2 mentions domain-incremental learning but the experiments focus mainly on task-IL and class-IL settings.

weaknesses.point: The study uses only linear classifiers on top of frozen or fine-tuned PLMs, which may limit generality.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.1 states the backbone model is a PLM combined with a linear classifier, but does not explore more complex architectures.

weaknesses.point: Some experimental details such as hyperparameter tuning and training stability are deferred to appendices, limiting reproducibility.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.1 and Appendix C mention hyperparameter search but details are not fully integrated into the main text.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not evaluate the impact of buffer size or memory constraints on rehearsal methods in depth.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 5.1 briefly discusses buffer size effects but lacks a systematic study of memory efficiency trade-offs.

weaknesses.point: No comparison with generative replay or meta-learning based continual learning methods.
weaknesses.evidence: Appendix A mentions generative replay and meta-learning but these approaches are not empirically evaluated.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not provide qualitative analysis or case studies illustrating failure modes or successes.
weaknesses.evidence: The results focus on quantitative metrics without qualitative examples or error analysis.

weaknesses.point: The conclusions about the superiority of BERT and experience replay may be dataset-dependent and need further validation.
weaknesses.evidence: The three datasets used have specific characteristics; generalization to other NLP tasks is not demonstrated.

questions.question: Have the authors considered evaluating domain-incremental or task-agnostic continual learning settings to assess the generality of their findings?
questions.motivation: These settings are more realistic and challenging, and it is important to know if the conclusions hold beyond task-IL and class-IL.

questions.question: Can the authors provide more insights or hypotheses on why rehearsal-based methods outperform regularization-based methods for PLMs?
questions.motivation: Understanding the underlying reasons could guide the design of more effective CL algorithms.

questions.question: How sensitive are the results to the choice of buffer size and memory constraints for experience replay methods?
questions.motivation: Memory efficiency is critical in continual learning, and performance may degrade with smaller buffers.

questions.question: Would incorporating more complex classifiers or adapters on top of PLMs affect the observed forgetting patterns and CL performance?
questions.motivation: Linear classifiers may limit the representational capacity and affect the generality of the conclusions.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of theoretical analysis or explanation for why certain PLMs or CL methods perform better. (The paper primarily focuses on empirical results and probing without providing theoretical insights or formal justification.)
critique_points.point_002: Limited exploration of domain-incremental or task-agnostic continual learning settings. (Section 2.2 mentions domain-incremental learning but the experiments focus mainly on task-IL and class-IL settings.)
critique_points.point_003: The study uses only linear classifiers on top of frozen or fine-tuned PLMs, which may limit generality. (Section 3.1 states the backbone model is a PLM combined with a linear classifier, but does not explore more complex architectures.)
critique_points.point_004: Some experimental details such as hyperparameter tuning and training stability are deferred to appendices, limiting reproducibility. (Section 3.1 and Appendix C mention hyperparameter search but details are not fully integrated into the main text.)
critique_points.point_005: The paper does not evaluate the impact of buffer size or memory constraints on rehearsal methods in depth. (Section 5.1 briefly discusses buffer size effects but lacks a systematic study of memory efficiency trade-offs.)
critique_points.point_006: No comparison with generative replay or meta-learning based continual learning methods. (Appendix A mentions generative replay and meta-learning but these approaches are not empirically evaluated.)
critique_points.point_007: The paper does not provide qualitative analysis or case studies illustrating failure modes or successes. (The results focus on quantitative metrics without qualitative examples or error analysis.)
critique_points.point_008: The conclusions about the superiority of BERT and experience replay may be dataset-dependent and need further validation. (The three datasets used have specific characteristics; generalization to other NLP tasks is not demonstrated.)
critique_points.point_009: Have the authors considered evaluating domain-incremental or task-agnostic continual learning settings to assess the generality of their findings? (These settings are more realistic and challenging, and it is important to know if the conclusions hold beyond task-IL and class-IL.)
critique_points.point_010: Can the authors provide more insights or hypotheses on why rehearsal-based methods outperform regularization-based methods for PLMs? (Understanding the underlying reasons could guide the design of more effective CL algorithms.)
critique_points.point_011: How sensitive are the results to the choice of buffer size and memory constraints for experience replay methods? (Memory efficiency is critical in continual learning, and performance may degrade with smaller buffers.)
critique_points.point_012: Would incorporating more complex classifiers or adapters on top of PLMs affect the observed forgetting patterns and CL performance? (Linear classifiers may limit the representational capacity and affect the generality of the conclusions.)
